topic: Yaa won a school prize — essay about farming

turns.content: my friend I have good news today. Yaa has won a prize at school
That's wonderful! Congratulations to Yaa. What did she win the prize for?
an essay competition. the topic was 'the work that feeds Ghana.' she wrote about cocoa farming. about our farm. the teachers say it was the best essay in her class

That must make you incredibly proud — she wrote about what she knows because she lives it every day. There's no better authority on cocoa farming than a girl who's grown up on the farm.

What kind of things did she write about? Did she share the essay with you?

she read it to us after dinner. she wrote about waking early to help turn the beans on the drying mat. about the smell of the cocoa when it ferments. she wrote that her father says cocoa is patient work, you plant today and your grandchildren harvest. I did not know she listened so carefully my friend

Children listen to far more than we think. She took your own words and wove them into her essay — that's both a good writer and a good daughter. The detail about the smell of fermenting cocoa is exactly the kind of thing that makes an essay come alive. A judge reads that and knows this girl has been there.

What was the prize?

a certificate and 3 exercise books. small things but she carries the certificate like it is made of gold. she showed every person in the compound. even old Nana Agyei who cannot read, she made him hold it

Ha — making Nana Agyei hold the certificate is the best part of the story. A prize is not about the thing itself but what it means to the person. Three exercise books and a certificate have made her feel like she conquered the world.

Does Yaa enjoy school generally? It sounds like she has a talent for writing.

she is a serious student. not like when I was a boy, always looking out the window wanting to go to the farm. Yaa sits and reads. she borrows books from the teacher. Akosua says she takes after her grandmother who was also sharp with words

It sounds like the gift skipped a generation and found its way back through Yaa. A child who borrows books from the teacher without being asked — that's self-motivated learning. You can't teach that.

Do they have more competitions like this at the school? If she did well in an essay about farming, she might do well in others too. Building confidence through these early wins matters a lot.
there is a district competition in June. the teacher says Yaa should enter. the topic is different but the teacher believes she can place well. I told Yaa if she enters I will be there to watch

That's a good teacher — recognizing talent and pushing a student forward. And knowing her father will be there watching is the kind of support that makes a child perform at her best.

If you want to help her prepare, you could practice together: let her write a draft, read it aloud to the family like she did with this one, and give her feedback. Reading aloud is how you catch awkward sentences and find the rhythm of good writing.
I like that idea. she can read to us after dinner like she did with this essay. Ama will also help, she is older and she reads well too. the sisters can work together

Ama helping Yaa is perfect — an older sister's feedback is different from a parent's. Ama can push her in ways that feel less like instruction and more like teamwork. And Yaa will want to impress her big sister, which will make her try harder.

Do you know what the district competition topic will be, or do they announce it closer to June?

they announce it one month before. so May we will know. but the teacher says it is always about something in Ghana, the culture or the land or the people. Yaa knows these things. 'one tree does not make a forest' — the sisters will prepare her together. medaase my friend. today is a good day. Nyame is kind

Today is a good day indeed. A daughter who turns her father's words into a winning essay — that is something to hold onto. Tell Yaa she should be proud, and good luck to her in the district competition in June. Good evening.
